summary:
On August 6 and 9, 1945, the United States dropped atomic bombs on the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, becoming the only nation in history to use nuclear weapons in warfare. The bomb dropped on Hiroshima, nicknamed "Little Boy," killed an estimated 70,000 to 80,000 people instantly and tens of thousands more in the weeks that followed from radiation sickness and burns. Three days later, the "Fat Man" bomb destroyed much of Nagasaki, killing between 40,000 and 80,000 people immediately. The total death toll from both bombings reached approximately 200,000 by the end of 1945.

The decision to use atomic weapons was made by President Harry S. Truman following consultations with military and scientific advisors. Proponents argued that the bombings would force Japan's surrender and avert the massive casualties expected from a conventional invasion of the Japanese home islands, Operation Downfall. Japan had demonstrated willingness to fight to the last in battles at Iwo Jima and Okinawa, and the military anticipated hundreds of thousands of Allied and millions of Japanese casualties in an invasion. The Soviet Union's declaration of war against Japan on August 8 further isolated Japanese leadership.

Japan's Emperor Hirohito announced surrender on August 15, 1945, citing "a new and most cruel bomb" as a factor in the decision. Formal surrender documents were signed aboard the USS Missouri on September 2, ending [[event:112|World War II]] in the Pacific. The bombings thus achieved their immediate military objective, though historians continue to debate whether they were morally justified and whether Japan would have surrendered without them.

The bombings were developed by the [[event:2765|Manhattan Project]], a secret wartime research program involving thousands of scientists, engineers, and workers across the United States, Canada, and the United Kingdom. The theoretical physics underlying the weapon traced to [[event:2701|Einstein's theory of relativity]] and work by nuclear physicists including Enrico Fermi, J. Robert Oppenheimer, and Niels Bohr.

The destruction of Hiroshima and Nagasaki inaugurated the nuclear age and transformed geopolitics. The USSR accelerated its own weapons program, successfully testing its first atomic bomb in 1949. This parity gave the [[event:113|Cold War]] its defining character — a standoff in which the major powers possessed weapons capable of mutual annihilation, a doctrine known as Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD). Fear of nuclear war shaped every major confrontation of the postwar era, including the [[event:2675|Cuban Missile Crisis]] of 1962.

The hibakusha — survivors of the bombings — faced lasting physical consequences from radiation exposure, including elevated rates of leukemia and other cancers, as well as severe social stigma in postwar Japan. Their testimony and advocacy became central to the global anti-nuclear movement, contributing to the 1963 [[event:3360|Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty]] and subsequent nonproliferation agreements. The cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki rebuilt and became symbols of peace, hosting annual memorials and maintaining museums dedicated to the victims and the dangers of nuclear weapons.
